items.prompt: Should apps be required to work well without tracking user behavior, even if that reduces convenience?

ChatGPT.reasoning: Apps should prioritize user privacy and function well without tracking, as convenience should not come at the cost of personal data security and trust. Users deserve control over their information.
ChatGPT.reasoningPreview: Apps should prioritize user privacy and function well without tracking, as convenience should not come at the cost of personal data security and trust. Users deserve control over their information.

Claude.reasoning: Yes, apps should be required to work without tracking. Privacy is a fundamental right that shouldn't be sacrificed for minor convenience gains, and tracking often serves corporate interests more than user needs.
Claude.reasoningPreview: Yes, apps should be required to work without tracking. Privacy is a fundamental right that shouldn't be sacrificed for minor convenience gains, and tracking often serves corporate interests more than user needs.

Gemini.reasoning: Yes. User privacy is a fundamental right, and convenience should not come at the cost of constant surveillance. Apps can be designed with privacy-preserving features.
Gemini.reasoningPreview: Yes. User privacy is a fundamental right, and convenience should not come at the cost of constant surveillance. Apps can be designed with privacy-preserving features.

Grok.reasoning: Yes, privacy rights outweigh convenience gains from tracking; apps must innovate to function well without surveillance.
Grok.reasoningPreview: Yes, privacy rights outweigh convenience gains from tracking; apps must innovate to function well without surveillance.

items.prompt: Should apps be required to work well without tracking user behavior, even if that reduces convenience?

ChatGPT.reasoning: Apps should be required to work well without tracking to uphold user privacy and autonomy, regardless of convenience loss. Privacy principles take precedence over optional tracking benefits.
ChatGPT.reasoningPreview: Apps should be required to work well without tracking to uphold user privacy and autonomy, regardless of convenience loss. Privacy principles take precedence over optional tracking benefits.

Claude.reasoning: Tracking enables personalization that measurably improves user experience and app quality, but privacy harms (surveillance, data breaches, discrimination) affect billions. The net depends on enforcement: if privacy regs exist, tracking's marginal convenience gain (~5-10% UX) doesn't justify residual harms; if unregulated, tracking wins on pure utility.

Grok.reasoning: Yes, requiring apps to function well without tracking spurs innovation in on-device AI and privacy tech, outweighing minor convenience trade-offs—inaction risks a surveillance dystopia.
